Article Title: Quote of the day by Microsoft AI CEO Mustafa Suleyman: “I think everyone gets a little bit caught up in the week-to-week, day-to-day, or the definitions of these abstract ideas. Just focus on…”
Article Text:
Microsoft AI CEO Mustafa Suleyman

Quote of the day by Mustafa Suleyman

“I think everyone gets a little bit caught up in the week-to-week, day-to-day, or the definitions of these abstract ideas. Just focus on the capabilities. You know, we should really be thinking about these things as Artificial Capable Intelligence.

What can it do in practice, and what is the value of that “doing”? I prefer that as a framing versus AGI, because it’s … more measurable, and we can actually look at it very, very explicitly in terms of its economic impact and its impact on work.

”

The quote that shifts the conversation

What Mustafa Suleyman’s quote mean in simple terms

Why AI definitions often create confusion

A practical way to understand AI

How AI is already part of everyday life

The impact on jobs and workplaces

Measuring AI through real results

The importance of the “value of doing”

AI across different industries

A shift in how AI is being discussed

Understanding AI in a simpler way

Final insight

Poll Which application of AI do you find most beneficial? Customer service chatbots Personalized shopping recommendations Data analysis tools





Artificial intelligence is no longer something that only scientists and tech experts talk about in labs or at conferences. It has slowly become a part of daily life, changing how people work, talk to each other, shop, and even learn. AI is already having an impact on many fields, from automated chat systems to tools that help with writing and analysing data. But even though AI is becoming more common, the way people talk about it often seems hard to understand and full of technical terms. People talk a lot about terms like "artificial general intelligence" (AGI), even though they don't really exist yet. In this case, a recent comment by Mustafa Suleyman, the CEO of Microsoft AI, makes AI easier to understand in a more realistic way. He says that instead of thinking about definitions or what AI systems might be able to do in the future, we should look at what they can do right now and how those actions add value.This statement takes the conversation from theory to reality. It brings up a simple but important point: instead of arguing about what AI might become, it's better to know what it can do right now.The quote is really about being clear. Suleyman says that when people talk about AI, they often get sidetracked by complicated definitions and predictions. In doing so, they ignore what AI can already do today."Artificial Capable Intelligence" is not a technical term for him; it's a way of thinking that works. It looks at how well something works in the real world instead of how well it could work in theory. In short, he wants people to judge AI by what it does.This means looking at how well AI does its job, how accurate it is, and how much value it adds. This makes AI easier to understand and easier to measure. The focus changes from thinking about what might happen in the future to what is happening right now.There have always been many different and sometimes unclear definitions of artificial intelligence. Artificial General Intelligence is one of the most talked-about terms. It means a system that can do any intellectual task that a person can do. But no one has made such a system yet, and experts are still arguing about what it would actually look like.This is why talks about AI can get abstract. People may talk about things that aren't real yet, which can make it hard to understand how technology is right now. Suleyman's statement directly addresses this issue by saying that these definitions are not the best way to understand AI today.By focusing on capability, Suleyman's approach makes the discussion easier. He says that instead of asking if AI is smart enough, we should ask what it can do right now.This means knowing how AI works in real life. For instance, can it quickly look at a lot of data? Can it help you make decisions or write? Can it do the same thing over and over again?Answering these questions makes it easier to see how far AI has come already. This method also makes the technology easier for people who don't have a technical background to understand.People don't always notice it, but AI is already being used in a lot of different ways. It powers streaming platforms' recommendation systems, helps filter out spam emails, and supports customer service through chat-based tools.AI is being used in offices to do things like entering data, making schedules, and doing basic analysis. This lets workers focus on tasks that require them to be creative and think critically.These examples show that AI is useful because it can do certain tasks quickly and well. It may not be as smart as a person in every way, but its ability to do some things is already making a difference.The effect of AI on work is one of the most important things Suleyman says. AI systems are getting better at doing things, and this is changing how work is done in many fields.AI is often used to help people do their jobs instead of taking them away. It can take care of tasks that are boring or take a long time, so workers can focus on more important things. This means that people will have to do different jobs instead of losing their jobs completely.AI tools can answer simple questions in customer service, while human agents can handle more complicated ones. AI can quickly process large datasets in fields that rely on data, which helps professionals make decisions more quickly.One important point in the quote is that AI should be judged by how well it works. It's better to look at what AI can do than to worry about whether it has reached a certain level of intelligence.This includes things like how much time it saves, how accurate its results are, and how it makes things run more smoothly. These are clear signs that can be seen and measured.This is especially important for businesses. It lets them know if putting money into AI is a good idea and how it helps them be more productive and grow.Suleyman's mention of the "value of doing" shows how important it is to take action. AI isn't useful just because it's a concept; it has to do something useful for it to be useful.This means looking at how AI helps people solve problems, make things better, and get real benefits. A clear and measurable benefit of an AI system is that it cuts down on the time it takes to do a task.Focusing on these results makes the conversation about AI more useful and relevant to daily life.AI's abilities are already clear in many fields. AI is being used in healthcare to help with diagnosis and look at medical data. In education, it helps make learning experiences that are unique to each student.AI systems are used in finance to find fraud and better manage risks. AI tools help with writing, editing, and recommending content in the media and content fields.All of these apps show that AI is useful because it can do certain tasks well. These are actual, quantifiable skills that correspond with Suleyman's viewpoint.People are talking about AI in a different way these days. In the past, people often talked about long-term possibilities and what might happen in the future. There is now more of a focus on real-world effects and current uses.This change is shown in Suleyman's statement. The conversation becomes more concrete and easier to follow when it focuses on abilities. It also shows people how AI is already changing their lives.This change is important because it helps bring technical experts and regular people closer together.One of the best things about Suleyman's method is that it makes a complicated subject easier to understand. By concentrating on AI's capabilities, it eliminates the necessity to comprehend intricate definitions.This makes AI easier for more people to use. People don't need to know all the technical details to see how it affects them. They can just look at how it's being used and what good things it does.This is very important now that AI is becoming a bigger part of everyday life.Mustafa Suleyman's quote gives us a useful way to think about AI in a world that is changing quickly. He shifts the focus from definitions to capabilities, which brings out what really matters: how AI systems affect the world.This method tells people to focus on concrete results and real-world uses instead of getting lost in abstract ideas. It gives a better idea of how AI is changing industries, jobs, and the value it brings.This point of view is helpful as AI keeps changing. It reminds us that the real value of technology isn't what it's called, but what it can do.
Article Summary: Mustafa Suleyman, the CEO of Microsoft AI, makes AI easier to understand in a more realistic way. He says that when people talk about AI, they often get sidetracked by complicated definitions and predictions. In doing so, they ignore what AI can already do today.

Article Title: Trump Phone gets a facelift, sheds its ‘Made in USA’ claim
Article Text:
Trump Mobile has updated its gold T1 Phone with a new design and specifications, including a triple camera and AMOLED display. However, the 'Made in USA' claim has softened to 'shaped by American innovation.' Priced at $499 with a $100 deposit, the phone's launch timeline remains a mystery, with a 'Join the Waitlist' prompt replacing any release date.

Trump Phone 's " Made in USA " promise has quietly evaporated

T1 Phone pricing stays at $499, for now

Watch Trump Mobile Launch Ignites Meme Storm | Patriotic Power Move Or Just Another Cash Grab? Trump Mobile Launch Ignites Meme Storm | Patriotic Power Move Or Just Another Cash Grab?





Trump Mobile has overhauled its website and revealed a refreshed design for the T1 Phone, but the golden smartphone remains as elusive as ever with no launch timeline in sight.The redesigned handset is still gold—because of course it is—now featuring a triple vertical camera array, a cleaner American flag on the rear, and a "Trump Mobile" wordmark on the camera bump. Gone is the oversized "T1" branding that plastered the back of earlier renders. The phone looks largely identical to what Trump Mobile executives Eric Thomas and Don Hendrickson showed The Verge over a video call back in February, now rendered in higher resolution on the revamped site.Specs have been updated too. The T1 now lists a 6.78-inch AMOLED display with a 120Hz refresh rate, a 50MP main camera alongside an 8MP ultrawide and 50MP 2x telephoto, a 50MP selfie camera, 512GB storage, a 5,000mAh battery with 30W charging, and an unspecified Qualcomm Snapdragon 7 series chip running Android 15.The "Made in USA" branding, which was the phone's original headline claim at launch in June 2025, has been progressively walked back with each website update. The T1 is now described as "shaped by American innovation" with "American teams helping guide design and quality"—a long way from the original pitch.Executives previously told The Verge that final assembly would happen in Miami, with most manufacturing done in a "favored nation" overseas.The phone is listed at a "promotional price" of $499, with $100 deposits still being accepted. That price will rise after the promotional window closes, though Trump Mobile hasn't said by how much—only that it will stay under $1,000. More notably, any mention of a release date has been stripped from the site entirely, replaced with a simple "Join the Waitlist" prompt.
Article Summary: Trump Mobile has updated its website and revealed a refreshed design for the T1 Phone. However, the phone's launch timeline remains a mystery, with a 'Join the Waitlist' prompt replacing any release date. The phone is listed at a "promotional price" of $499, with $100 deposits still being accepted. The 'Made in USA' claim has softened to'shaped by American innovation'

Article Title: Tim Cook's glasses might be a clue to what Apple's smart glasses will look like
Article Text: Apple's smart glasses are coming in 2027, and Tim Cook's own frames might be a preview. Bloomberg's Mark Gurman reports the company is testing at least four styles for its first pair, codenamed N50—one of which mirrors the slim rectangular glasses Cook wears. Built from premium acetate and designed entirely in-house, the display-free glasses will lean heavily on an upgraded Siri, expected to arrive with iOS 27.
Article Summary: Apple is testing at least four styles for its first smart glasses. The glasses, codenamed N50, are expected to launch in 2027. They will be display-free and powered by an upgraded Siri. One of the styles mirrors the rectangular glasses Tim Cook wears.

Article Title: Vivo T5 Pro with 9,020 mAh battery and 90W fast charging support launched in India: Price, specs and more
Article Text:
Vivo T5 Pro: Price and availability



Vivo T5 Pro specifications







Vivo T5 Pro is now official. Vivo has expanded its T-series of smartphones with the launch of Vivo T5 Pro in India. The smartphone is powered by a Qualcomm chipset and features a 1.5K AMOLED display . Vivo T5 Pro is backed by a massive 9,020 mAh battery and the IP68 & IP69 rating makes the smartphone resistant to dust and water.With the T5 Pro, we’re elevating the Series T by delivering a dependable, high-performance smartphone built for today’s always-on consumers. As usage evolves with 4K content, larger apps, and AI-driven features, power demands have grown significantly. Featuring our biggest-ever battery, the T5 Pro addresses this need by reducing charge anxiety and enabling users to stay connected, create, and play without interruption—reflecting our commitment to enhancing everyday experiences,” said Pankaj Gandhi, chief business officer – Online, vivo India.The vivo T5 Pro 5G will be available in Glacier Blue and Cosmic Black, and will be priced at Rs 29,999 (8GB + 128GB, inclusive of all taxes), INR 33,999 (8GB + 256GB, inclusive of all taxes), and Rs 39,999 (12GB + 256GB, inclusive of all taxes). The smartphone will go on sale starting April 21, 2026, across Flipkart, vivo India e-store, and all partner retail stores.Consumers can avail of the following offers when the phone goes on sale*:* Up to Rs 3000 Instant Bank Discount ( HDFC, SBI, Axis ) / UPI, OR* Up to Rs 3000 Exchange Bonus* Up to 6 months of no-cost EMIVivo T5 Pro comes with a 6.83-inch AMOLED display with 1260x2800 pixel resolution.The display offers 144hz refresh rate and up to 1900 nits peak tightness levels. The smartphone is powered by an octa-core Qualcomm Snapdragon 7s Gen chipset. The smartphone packs up to 12GB of RAM and it comes in two storage options — 128GB and 256GB.Vivo T5 Pro runs Android 16 operating system topped with company’s own layer of OriginOS 16. The smartphone is equipped with a 50MP Sony IMX882 main camera, paired with a 32MP front camera for sharp, detailed selfies. AI-powered features such as AI Erase13 and AI UHD13 further refine images by removing unwanted elements and enhancing clarity, delivering polished, professional-looking results with minimal effort.Vivo T5 Pro houses a massive 9,020 mAh battery which comes with 90W FlashCharge support. The company claims that the smartphone can charge up to 50% charge in just 37 minutes.
Article Summary: Vivo T5 Pro will be available in Glacier Blue and Cosmic Black. The smartphone will go on sale starting April 21, 2026, across Flipkart, vivo India e-store, and all partner retail stores. The phone is equipped with a 50MP Sony IMX882 main camera, paired with a 32MP front camera for sharp, detailed selfies.

Article Title: Jesse Dwyer: Perplexity AI Chief Communications Officer Jesse Dwyer may have a 'message' for Google: Web search is 'primitive technology' and has not seen any innovation for 24 years
Article Text:
Perplexity believes the real breakthrough lies in technology itself



Perplexity argues that AI is more than just an upgrade



Ads, monetisation, and target users







The next battle in search may not be about ads or user behaviour, but about rewriting the technology itself. That’s the message from Perplexity AI, which is now positioning its platform as a fundamental rethink of how information is retrieved online. According to a report by Benzinga, Jesse Dwyer, Chief Communications Officer at Perplexity, said: “Search, as most people know it, is a primitive technology that didn’t experience any real innovation for 24 years.” He added that the arrival of AI-native tools made the disruption “obvious.”While most of the industry debate is centred around user behaviour and monetisation, but Perplexity believes that the real breakthrough lies in technology itself. The company frames that the web as the world’s largest hard drive, where the “write” function has long been solved, but the “read” function has lagged. AI, it says, finally makes that read function possible.Perplexity sees AI as more than just an upgrade to queries. The traditional systems take instructions, whereas the AI systems take objectives. Instead of returning links, they aim to deliver direct answers, reshaping what users expect from computing.“As the computer is now evolving, what users ask and do with it also evolves,” Dwyer explained.Unlike its rivals like Google , OpenAI and Meta, which are also exploring ad-driven models, Perplexity it is focusing on serving the “curious decision-makers whose choices can be “GDP-altering or history-making.” Dwyer noted: “It seems reasonable to assume we should have no problem making money with those people as our most passionate users.”Early hype branded Perplexity as a “Google killer,” but the company is distancing itself from that narrative. Instead, it highlights its edge in accurate AI and “massively multi-model orchestration,” which it claims go beyond simply being AI-native.
Article Summary: Perplexity AI is positioning its platform as a fundamental rethink of how information is retrieved online. The company frames the web as the world’s largest hard drive, where the ‘write’ function has long been solved, but the “read” function has lagged.

Article Title: Netflix unveils ‘VOID’, an AI model that can change a movie plot
Article Text:
Netflix

How Netflix VOID works







Netflix has unveiled an AI model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, Netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next, essentially altering the causality. Most object-removal technology leaves a gap where something used to be and/ or fills it with a plausible-looking background. VOID goes a step further: after erasing an object from a scene, it calculates how the surrounding environment should realistically respond to its absence, and in the process it adjusts the behaviour of other elements in the frame accordingly.The model, called VOID, is a vision-language system designed to edit video footage with a level of contextual awareness that sets it apart from several existing tools.For example, imagine this scenario: A movie director has just wrapped up a multi-million dollar action sequence where the star drives straight into a fiery, head-on collision. The cars explode, debris scatters, and the scene is a wrap.But suddenly, there is request to change the scene: The character survives and drive off into the sunset instead. This would mean costly reshoots or millions spent on computer-generated graphics.Netflix’s VOID can alter the scene and digitally erase the collision, the second vehicle, the smoke, and the flying debris. It will then generate new, realistic footage of pristine pavement, making it look as though the main character simply drove down an empty, peaceful road.The AI’s ‘magic’ lies in its grasp of real-world physics. Let us understand this by a different example. If a video shows a person jumping into a pool and splashing water onto the deck, VOID can completely erase the person. It will then intelligently alter the water and the wet concrete, leaving behind a perfectly still, undisturbed pool as if nobody had ever jumped in.“To train the model, we generate a new paired dataset of counterfactual object removals using Kubric and HUMOTO, where removing an object requires altering downstream physical interactions. During inference, a vision-language model identifies regions of the scene affected by the removed object. These regions are then used to guide a video diffusion model that generates physically consistent counterfactual outcomes,” Netflix said.“Experiments on both synthetic and real data show that our approach better preserves consistent scene dynamics after object removal compared to prior video object removal methods,” the company added.Netflix isn’t keeping this powerful tool locked behind studio doors, and made the VOID model available to the public on the AI platform Hugging Face, allowing anyone to download and use it.While there are already several video-altering tools on the market, such as Runway, DiffuEraser, and ProPainter, the Netflix team claims VOID is vastly superior, as per The Register.
Article Summary: Netflix has unveiled an AI model that can alter the scene. While this is nothing new, Netflix’s model not just remove objects from video, it understands what should happen next, essentially altering the causality. The model, called VOID, is a vision-language system designed to edit video footage with a level of contextual awareness.

Article Title: Microsoft: Tech Layoffs: Layoffs at Microsoft, Meta, Google, Twitter: What is 'career cushioning' and why it's important
Article Text:
As job cuts continue to rise in various industries, some employees are going for 'career cushioning'.

Q: What is career cushioning?

Q: Why is career cushioning important?

Q: How can I start career cushioning?

Q: What are some examples of career cushioning?

Q: Is career cushioning only for people in certain industries?

Q: How often should I review and update my career cushioning efforts?

Q: How do I know if my career cushioning efforts are working?

Q: How much time should I be dedicating to career cushioning?

NEW DELHI: Microsoft Corp is reportedly planning to cut thousands of jobs, with some roles expected to be eliminated in the human resources and engineering divisions, according to media reports on Tuesday.This move follows similar job cuts announced by other tech companies, such as Amazon, Meta Platforms , Twitter, Google , Salesforce and a host of other corporations.These job cuts are being attributed to slowing demand and a worsening global economic outlook. The move by Microsoft indicates that the tech sector may continue to shed jobs in the coming months.As job cuts continue to rise in tech and various other industries, some employees are choosing to proactively seek out new job opportunities while still employed. This strategy, referred to as " career cushioning ". Here is all you need to know on 'career cushioning'A: Career cushioning refers to the practice of building a diverse set of skills and experiences in order to make oneself more marketable and resilient to job loss or industry changes. This can include gaining experience in multiple areas of a field, developing a side hustle or passion project, or building a professional network.A: Career cushioning can help provide a sense of security and confidence in uncertain job markets and can also open up new opportunities for career advancement. It can also help you be better prepared if you do lose your job, making it easier to find new employment.A: There are several ways to begin career cushioning, such as:Building a diverse skill set through continuing education or volunteer workNetworking with professionals in your industry and other industriesBuilding a personal brand through social media and professional online presenceSaving money for unexpected career changes or job loss.A: Some examples of career cushioning include:-A software developer taking on freelance web design projects to gain experience in user interface design.-A marketing professional starting a blog to gain experience in content creation.-A sales representative taking on additional responsibilities in product development to gain experience in product management.A: No, career cushioning is applicable for people in any industry. It's about being proactive in preparing for potential changes in your career, regardless of the industry you're in.A: It's a good idea to review your career cushioning efforts regularly, perhaps once or twice a year. This will allow you to assess your progress and make any necessary adjustments to your plans. Additionally, keeping an eye on the job market and industry trends can help you anticipate and prepare for any potential changes in your field.A: You can gauge the effectiveness of your career cushioning efforts by monitoring your progress in gaining new skills and experiences, as well as the feedback you receive from your professional network and potential employers. Additionally, you may find that you are better able to adapt to changes in your industry, or that you have more opportunities available to you.A: The amount of time you should dedicate to career cushioning will depend on your individual goals and circumstances, however it's important to balance the time you spend career cushioning with your other responsibilities. A good rule of thumb is to spend some time each week or month working on career cushioning activities, but not to the point that it negatively impacts other areas of your life.
Article Summary: As job cuts continue to rise in various industries, some employees are going for 'career cushioning' Career cushioning refers to the practice of building a diverse set of skills and experiences in order to make oneself more marketable. This can include gaining experience in multiple areas of a field, developing a side hustle or passion project, or building a professional network.

Article Title: Sahara discovery: 7,000-year-old skeletons with unknown DNA puzzle scientists
Article Text:
PC: AI-Generated

Sahara skeletons provide genetic evidence of an extinct human group

What makes this DNA different from that of modern humans

Life in the Green Sahara

Why this discovery matters for human evolution



Sahara DNA study reveals untold story of early human populations







The discovery of fossilised human remains in the Sahara Desert has marked a new era in research on the history of human evolution. Researchers have discovered skeletons dating back 7,000 years that do not belong to any existing lineage. This discovery highlights unknown human lineages that existed in the African continent but went extinct without leaving any traces of their existence.The fossilised human remains were discovered in the heart of a desert; however, in its prime, the Sahara was home to several communities of humans. This phase of history, popularly known as the Green Sahara, witnessed human migration, hunting, and settlement within territories that remain uninhabited today. The analysis of the DNA has provided new insights into human evolution during this period.These skeletons have been unearthed from different archaeological locations in the Sahara and can be dated back to about 7,000 years ago. The scientists have analysed the DNA samples of these skeletons in comparison with those of living human beings.The research “ Ancient DNA from the Green Sahara reveals ancestral North African lineage ”, published in the journal Nature, gives an analysis of the genetics of these people.According to the analysis conducted in this paper, it turns out that these ancient people belong to a certain lineage of people that no more existing in today’s world.An important feature regarding this particular discovery is that their genetics do not seem to correspond to any of the currently existing populations. Although modern humans are all related, it seems that this group of people had its own distinct genetic profile.This does not imply that this was a different species altogether; rather, it implies that these people belonged to a lineage that evolved separately from others. With time, this particular lineage dwindled or intermixed with other populations, leaving behind little evidence in our genetic make-up.The Sahara Desert was not always a desert. Roughly 7,000 years ago, it had lakes, plant life, and animal life, which made it a friendly environment to live in.These ancient humans most likely depended on hunting, gathering, and fishing to survive. The presence of water bodies and fertile land would enable them to form societies.Nevertheless, due to changes in the climatic conditions of the area, making it dry, these humans had to migrate or adapt, which might have led to a genetic dilution.This discovery is important because it shows the intricacy of the history of humans, which turns out to be more complex than was first thought. Instead of moving in a linear progression, human evolution involved interaction, separation, and even extinction of different populations.A new lineage means that other lineages could exist but have yet to be discovered. The discovery of this lineage also shows that Africa had many different and organised groups living there long before any written history could be recorded. This information can help determine how people migrated over time.This finding that people living 7,000 years ago in the Sahara possessed unique DNA brings into question many theories regarding human origin. It demonstrates the presence of different groups in ancient Africa that could not survive till nowadays.What is more important, it makes it clear that the history of the human race has not yet been written completely. Each new piece of evidence helps us discover new facts about our past.
Article Summary: Researchers have discovered skeletons dating back 7,000 years that do not belong to any existing lineage. This discovery highlights unknown human lineages that existed in the African continent but went extinct without leaving any traces of their existence. The analysis of the DNA has provided new insights into human evolution during this period.

Article Title: World'S Deepest Rivers: 10 Deepest rivers in the world ranked: From the Congo’s 250m depths to the Mississippi river
Article Text:
PC: World Atlas

List of the deepest rivers in the world



World’s deepest rivers : From Congo to the Mississippi River



Congo River (~250 m)



Yangtze River (~200 m)



Danube River (~178 m)



Zambezi River (~116 m)



Mekong River (~100 m)



Amazon River (~91 m)



Yellow River (~80 m)



Hudson River (~66 m)



Saint Lawrence River (~65 m)



Mississippi River (~61 m)









Rivers create landforms, foster biodiversity, and provide sustenance to humanity; however, little attention is paid to their depth. The world's deepest rivers showcase the other face of these mighty water bodies. Below the tranquil waters lie several rivers that reach hundreds of meters into the Earth, formed through geological processes, erosion, and massive water flows. Determining the depth of rivers may not be exact since water levels may fluctuate due to seasonal changes and precipitation. Nonetheless, scientists have come up with accurate maximum depth measures for large rivers. Below are the top 10 deepest rivers in the world according to World Geography. Rivers are ranked not by size or volume but by their depths.The Congo River is generally acknowledged to be the deepest river on earth, where depths of up to 250 meters have been recorded. The Congo River is a major river flowing through Central Africa and drains into the Atlantic Ocean carrying an immense quantity of water. The depth of the river can be attributed to the presence of sharp channels under the river and strong currents, which have carved out very deep parts of the river.The Yangtze River is the largest river in Asia and has a depth of about 200 meters in some areas. It is deepest where the Three Gorges are located. In those locations, the Yangtze passes through steep gorges bordered by high mountains. Naturally, this increases the depth greatly. This river has great importance in terms of transport, power generation, and water provision.Danube River is among the major rivers in Europe with maximum depths reaching 178 meters. The Danube River runs through many nations and acts as an important water passage. Deeper areas are typically located in valleys and constrictions formed by long-term geological changes. The river's depths facilitate transport and trading activities between many European towns.The Zambezi River reaches a maximum depth of about 116 metres. The river is located in the region of southern Africa and features some of the most striking formations of nature, such as Victoria Falls. The depth of the river is attributed to the presence of canyons and falls that have been formed due to erosion and the rapid flow of water through the river.The Mekong River flows through some countries in Southeast Asia and has a depth of about 100 meters. It has been observed that this river has a great ecosystem. Monsoons have a significant effect on the river’s depth, as its depth changes from time to time due to these rains. The river serves an important purpose in agriculture and fishing.The Amazon River reaches depths of about 91 metres and carries more water than any other river in the world. It drains the largest river basin globally. The depth varies depending on rainfall and seasonal flooding, especially during the wet season. Its immense volume of water contributes to its depth, making it one of the most significant river systems on Earth.The Yellow River nicknamed “Huang He,” has a depth of up to 80 meters. It transports some of the largest amounts of sediments found in any rivers, affecting their composition and depth. Sediment buildup causes an increase in elevation of the riverbed, thus making the river deeper. Activities carried out by people, such as irrigation, affect the depth of the rivers as well.The depth of the Hudson River is around 66 meters at most. The river runs through New York, and it is characterized by a fjord-like appearance at some points. The geologic feature of the Hudson River makes it possible to have deep waters, especially at West Point.The Saint Lawrence River connects the Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean, and its deepest point is 65 meters. Freshwater in large quantities is transported through the Saint Lawrence River, thus keeping the river deep throughout. The Saint Lawrence River was formed due to the glaciers digging into the earth.The Mississippi River is up to 61 meters deep in the lower part of its course. This river runs through the USA and carries away the waters of a large watershed. The most profound points occur after the confluence with the Ohio River. All the time, sediment transportation forms new shapes of the riverbed.
Article Summary: The Congo River is generally acknowledged to be the deepest river on earth, where depths of up to 250 meters have been recorded. The Yangtze River is the largest river in Asia and has a depth of about 200 meters in some areas. The Amazon River reaches depths of about 91 metres and carries more water than any other river in the world.

Article Title: Paid to exist: Japanese man earns $80K a year just by renting himself out to lonely people
Article Text:
Turning existence into companionship for lonely people

Why people hire him

Avoid doing activities alone

Sit quietly with them during difficult moments

Attend events where going solo feels uncomfortable

Experience companionship without emotional pressure

A reflection of modern loneliness

A simple idea with global attention







In a world where hustle culture dominates, one man in Japan has built a career by doing the exact opposite. Shoji Morimoto has gained global attention for offering a unique service: he rents himself out to strangers simply to be present. Charging around $70 per session, Morimoto accompanies clients to meals, walks or events without offering conversation, advice or emotional involvement. His approach may sound unusual, but it has struck a chord in modern urban life, where loneliness is rising and many people seek connection without pressure or expectations.Morimoto launched his service in 2018 after leaving a job where he felt unfulfilled and underutilised. Instead of chasing traditional work, he leaned into the idea that simply being present could have value.His offering is deliberately minimal. He does not entertain, advise or actively engage unless necessary. Clients hire him for his quiet presence, whether that means sitting beside them during a meal or accompanying them to an appointment.What makes the concept stand out is its honesty. Morimoto openly states that he provides “nothing” beyond availability, which has become the core appeal of his service.Morimoto typically charges a fixed fee of around $60 to $70 per session, with additional travel expenses covered by the client. At peak demand, he has received more than 100 requests per month.While estimates suggest he earns around $80K annually, this figure is not officially confirmed and may vary depending on demand and workload. Even so, his business model has proven sustainable, showing that unconventional ideas can generate steady income.Clients turn to Morimoto for a wide range of reasons, many of which reflect subtle social needs rather than practical ones.Some hire him to:In many cases, conversation is minimal or absent. The comfort comes from simply having another person nearby, without expectations.Morimoto’s success highlights a broader social trend, particularly in urban Japan, where isolation has become increasingly common. Busy lifestyles, shrinking social circles and cultural factors have contributed to a growing sense of disconnection.His service offers a middle ground between complete solitude and traditional relationships. It allows people to feel accompanied without the obligations that often come with friendships or social interactions.One of the most important aspects of Morimoto’s work is what he does not offer. He does not position himself as a friend, counsellor or companion in the emotional sense.Instead, he provides neutral presence. This distinction is key to understanding why his service works. Clients are not seeking deep connection but rather a shared moment that requires no effort to maintain.Since gaining popularity, Morimoto’s story has been covered by media outlets around the world. His unconventional approach has sparked conversations about work, value and human connection in the modern age.While the idea of getting paid to “do nothing” may seem surprising, it reveals a deeper truth about contemporary life: sometimes, presence alone can be enough.
Article Summary: Shoji Morimoto rents himself out to strangers simply to be present. Charging around $70 per session, Morimoto accompanies clients to meals, walks or events without offering conversation, advice or emotional involvement. His approach may sound unusual, but it has struck a chord in modern urban life.

Article Title: Japan deep-sea discovery: 38 new species found 5,000 meters deep inside a mysterious ‘glass castle’
Article Text:
PC: Google Gemini

How ‘Glass Castle’ sponges support a rare deep-sea ecosystem

How DNA sequencing identified 38 new species



Why these specimens are changing marine science



How life coexists inside glass sponges







A groundbreaking advancement in marine biology occurred with the identification of 38 new marine species collected from the deep waters around Japan! The research, conducted by the Nippon Foundation - Nekton Ocean Census and JAMSTEC as part of their ongoing expeditionary program, began with the examination of the Nankai Trough and Shichiyo Seamount Chain in 2025. Approximately 5,000 meters below sea level, researchers discovered the ‘Glass Castle’ comprised of the delicate silica skeletons of hexactinellid sponges. These shimmery sponges provide living space for organisms that had never been recorded before.Using cutting-edge submersibles (manned and unmanned) and DNA sequence technology, researchers were able to evaluate an area that comprises one of the last frontiers of our planet to be evaluated for marine biodiversity - the deep sea. The results demonstrated that the complexity and abundance of life in the deep sea are far greater than anyone has ever anticipated. The study underscores the necessity for global ocean conservation as the evidence of vulnerability of these ecosystems necessitates immediate global protection. This discovery highlights the urgent need to safeguard these fragile, alien landscapes before human activity disrupts their ancient biological harmony.The ‘Glass Castle’ provides the physical foundation needed to support the symbiotic relationship between hexactinellid sponges and less-known organisms. According to Ocean Census, the lesser-known organisms include polychaete worms, two species of which were discovered during this study: Dalhousiella yabukii and Leocratides watanabeae. The benefits provided by the sponges (protection) have resulted in the establishment of an incredibly rare and delicate invertebrate community exhibiting the ability to survive at great depths.According to the study published in JAMSTEC, the team used the Research Vessel Yokosuka and the Shinkai 6500 submersible to explore depths down to 5,000-meters under water. They used ‘cyber-taxonomy,’ which combines rapid DNA sequencing with high-resolution imaging, to quickly establish that they located 38 new species within a few weeks instead of the years that would typically be required for that kind of classification. They were able to explore five previously unilluminated volcanic peaks and abyssal trenches.This mission was part of a larger effort to close the taxonomic gap. Scientists found they recorded five times more species of biodiversity in the area of these specific Japanese abyssal trenches than previously documented. In addition to the 38 new species found, the team also collected over 500 total organisms, as noted in Ocean Decade; many of those specimens are still being analysed through genomic sequencing. Studies like this are important for discovering how forms of life exist in extreme conditions of pressure and total darkness.They looked specifically at how species living in the abyss interacted with each other. The investigators observed a variety of previously unknown worms and crustaceans that only live within the interior of glass sponges, which indicates a high degree of specialised evolutionary function. These organisms have adapted to live under the immense pressure of the Nankai Trough by establishing very close biological relationships with one another, whereby sponges afford them shelter and, in return, they may perform nutrient recycling or scavenging-related activities for the sponges.
Article Summary: DNA sequencing identified 38 new species of marine life in the Japanese abyssal trenches. The ‘Glass Castle’ provides the physical foundation needed to support the symbiotic relationship between hexactinellid sponges and less-known organisms. The study underscores the necessity for global ocean conservation as the evidence of vulnerability of these ecosystems requires immediate global protection.

Article Title: Operation Cat Drop: It’s raining cats in Malaysia: The story of ‘Operation Cat Drop’
Article Text:
PC: Google Gemini







‘Operation Cat Drop’ in Malaysia explained

Why were cats dropped from the sky



Lessons from Operation Cat Drop







As the title suggests, Operation Cat Drop appears to be a fairy tale. However, it really took place in the 1950s when a series of bizarre occurrences in Borneo resulted in cats being dropped from aeroplanes. The whole incident started with an innocent intention to help solve an environmental issue that soon escalated into a catastrophe. This story seems interesting, but also amusing and demonstrates the potential dangers of human interference in nature. Now, let us briefly examine how and why the felines fell from the sky. It also highlights the importance of carefully evaluating ecological solutions before implementing them on a large scale.The Operation Cat Drop took place in the 1950s in Sarawak, situated in Malaysia, which is an island nation known as Borneo. There was a malaria epidemic in the region that was due to the existence of mosquitoes. It was thought that the pesticide DDT would solve this mosquito problem.This strategy seemed promising as mosquitoes' populations decreased and the number of cases of malaria fell. Nevertheless, DDT also had adverse effects and influenced various organisms.Thus, insects that were consumed by geckos were destroyed; the geckos themselves died because of the chemical, and consequently, the cats which consumed the geckos became victims.As stated in the research from the Department of Occupational and Environmental Health, University of Iowa, Iowa City, "the insecticide DDT accumulated in the food chain, leading to considerable ecological disturbance." In addition, rats became numerous, with cats dying because of the poisoning.Due to the reduced number of cats, the population of rats increased dramatically. Such circumstances posed a real problem because rats could cause the outbreak of diseases, such as the plague and typhus. The ecosystem was unbalanced, and urgent actions had to be taken.The proposed decision may seem unconventional, but quite efficient: cats had to be introduced in the region once again. The process, however, was complicated because of the challenging geography. Therefore, an unprecedented strategy was elaborated: cats were put into crates and air-dropped into villages.Such an unconventional mission was called "Operation Cat Drop." Cats were air-dropped to restore ecological equilibrium. Most of the animals landed in good condition and started to control the rat population almost immediately.It might seem strange, but it actually worked. The population of rats was reduced, and the diseases stopped spreading.Operation Cat Drop is commonly cited in ecology and environmental science texts. It provides an illustration of how meddling with nature, even with the best intentions, might result in unintended outcomes.For example, the use of DDT was intended to solve one problem, but it gave rise to other issues. As some scientists pointed out, “the complex systems of nature can react unpredictably to human tampering.” Such statements are relevant to today’s environmental discussions and pesticide utilisation.Moreover, Operation Cat Drop emphasises the significance of recognising food chains and ecological equilibrium. Disruption in one organism's life can cause an imbalance elsewhere in the ecosystem.The case sounds amusing at first glance. However, it raises a vital question about the delicate balance of nature. Therefore, one should be careful while intervening with ecological processes.
Article Summary: Operation Cat Drop took place in the 1950s in Sarawak, situated in Malaysia, which is an island nation known as Borneo. The insecticide DDT accumulated in the food chain, leading to considerable ecological disturbance. In addition, rats became numerous, with cats dying because of the poisoning. The population of rats was reduced, and the diseases stopped spreading.

Article Title: Scientists reveal how Egypt’s Great Pyramid was built by moving 2.3 million stones without modern machines
Article Text:
How ancient Egyptians moved massive stones to build the Great Pyramid

A 3D model showing a hidden spiral ramp used to move stones up the pyramid.

Why earlier theories struggled

Clues hidden in the pyramid itself

Could it really have been built this fast?

What scientists still don’t agree on



A human achievement, not a mystery







More than 4,500 years after it was built, the Great Pyramid of Giza continues to puzzle scientists. Constructed during the reign of Khufu, the monument is made up of roughly 2.3 million stone blocks, some weighing several tonnes, all assembled with remarkable precision. For decades, experts have debated how ancient workers achieved this without cranes, steel, or modern tools. Now, a new study led by Vicente Luis Rosell Roig, published in the journal Nature , offers a compelling explanation that blends engineering logic with archaeological evidence, pointing to a hidden construction method embedded within the pyramid itself.At the heart of the research is a concept known as the Integrated Edge-Ramp (IER) system. Instead of building massive ramps outside the pyramid, which would have required enormous amounts of material and space, the study suggests that the ramp was built into the structure itself.Workers likely left intentional gaps along the outer layers of the pyramid. These gaps formed a spiralling pathway that allowed labourers to move stones upward as construction progressed. Once a section was completed, the gaps were filled in, leaving no visible trace of the ramp in the final structure.As Roig explains in the study, the goal was to test whether such a system could “achieve the documented construction rate within the Old Kingdom’s material and technological constraints.” In simple terms, the model asks whether ancient Egyptians could realistically have built the pyramid this way using the tools they actually had.The scale of the challenge is staggering. Each block had to be quarried, transported, lifted, and precisely placed. The new model suggests that workers used sledges, manpower, and carefully coordinated logistics, moving stones along the internal ramp system.Rather than lifting blocks vertically in one go, which would have been nearly impossible, the spiral pathway allowed gradual elevation. This reduced strain, distributed weight more efficiently, and kept the structure stable throughout construction.According to the simulation, blocks could have been positioned every four to six minutes during peak construction. When scaled across the entire project, this rate makes the pyramid’s completion within a few decades far more plausible than previously thought.For years, the dominant explanation involved large external ramps. However, these theories ran into serious problems. A straight ramp long enough to reach the pyramid’s top would have been enormous, potentially requiring more material than the pyramid itself.Other proposals suggested internal ramps, but they often failed to explain how workers managed tight turns or reached higher levels efficiently.Roig’s model stands out because it addresses multiple constraints at once. It accounts for limited space, manageable material use, structural stability, and realistic construction speed. It also aligns with what is known about ancient Egyptian tools and techniques.Archaeologists have long emphasised the ingenuity of ancient builders. As Egyptologist Mark Lehner has noted in previous research, “The pyramids were not built by slaves or by mystery, but by skilled workers using clever organisation and simple tools.”One of the most intriguing aspects of the theory is how it explains physical features that have puzzled researchers for years. Certain gaps, irregularities, and wear patterns within the pyramid may not be flaws at all, but remnants of the construction process.If confirmed, this would mean the pyramid itself functions as a record of its own construction, preserving evidence of ancient engineering decisions within its structure. It also suggests that what appears architecturally seamless on the outside may conceal a highly adaptive and evolving building strategy beneath the surface.The study suggests that areas showing signs of heavy use could have been pathways for moving materials, while inconsistencies in stone placement might reflect sections that were later filled in after serving as ramps.These details provide indirect support for the idea that the pyramid’s construction method was intentionally concealed as part of its final design.The timeline has always been a major point of debate. Historical estimates suggest the pyramid was completed in roughly 20 to 30 years. Critics have often questioned whether such a massive project could be finished so quickly.However, the new model supports this timeframe. By combining continuous ramp access with efficient labour organisation, the study shows that the construction pace could realistically match historical records.When quarrying, transport, and workforce logistics are included, the timeline still fits within the accepted range, strengthening the credibility of the theory.Despite its strengths, the Integrated Edge-Ramp theory is not universally accepted. Archaeology rarely provides definitive answers, and much of the evidence remains indirect.Some researchers point out that no fully preserved internal ramp has been directly observed. Others argue that multiple methods may have been used during different construction phases.As with many ancient mysteries, the truth may lie in a combination of techniques rather than a single solution.What this research ultimately reinforces is that the builders of the Great Pyramid of Giza did not rely on lost technology or supernatural help. Instead, they used ingenuity, planning, and an advanced understanding of materials and labour.The idea that the ramp was built into the pyramid itself reframes the entire construction process. It suggests that what appears to be a perfect, seamless monument is actually the result of a carefully hidden system that made the impossible possible.Even today, the pyramid stands as a reminder that ancient engineering was far more sophisticated than we often assume.
Article Summary: The Great Pyramid of Giza was built 4,500 years ago by Khufu. It is made up of roughly 2.3 million stone blocks, some weighing several tonnes. For decades, experts have debated how ancient workers achieved this without cranes, steel, or modern tools. A new model suggests that workers used sledges, manpower, and carefully coordinated logistics, moving stones along the internal ramp system.

Article Title: Nominee vs legal heir: What is the difference and who has claim on assets? All you need to know
Article Text:
AI image

Who is a Nominee?



Who is a Legal Heir?

Difference between a Nominee and a Legal heir

Aspect Nominee Legal Heir Role Custodian Owner Transfer of Assets Can be done without any legal formalities. Requires testamentary documents for proof of ownership. Powers No power to transact, use, or transfer assets. Power to use and transact assets as an owner. Appointment Owner can appoint anyone, but some institutions specify certain conditions. Determined by succession/personal laws. Legal Right Cannot contest ownership legally. Can contest ownership in a court of law.

When individuals open bank accounts, invest in mutual funds, or buy insurance, they are often asked to name a nominee.However, many people mistakenly assume that the nominee automatically inherits the assets. In reality, the nominee is not always the final recipient — the ownership rights belong to the legal heir, reported ET.Understanding the distinction between these two is essential to ensure that your wealth is passed on to the intended beneficiaries.A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets.The nominee’s role is limited to holding and managing the assets until they are legally transferred to the rightful heirs.According to ET, the nominee cannot sell, trade, or make any transactional decisions about the assets.The account or policy owner can appoint anyone — family, friends, or acquaintances — as a nominee, though financial institutions often prefer close family members.In some cases, the nominee and the legal heir can be the same person, which helps in ensuring a smoother transfer of assets.The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away.To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will or other testamentary proof.If there is no will — a situation known as dying intestate — the heir is determined as per the country’s succession or personal laws, reported ET.While a nominee can initially access the assets, only the legal heir can claim them permanently or challenge ownership in court.Experts quoted by ET emphasise that having clarity on this difference helps avoid disputes and ensures that the savings reach the intended family members or beneficiaries.Therefore, it is advisable to regularly update both nominees and wills to align with one’s estate planning goals.
Article Summary: A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets. The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away. To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will.
